text:
<@UNLS33M61> I've updated the programs so they are working again. The Eve 0.2-0.4 execution model was centered around relational algebra, and the heart of Eve was a generic join algorithm. Performance was always an issue with this model. We highlighted this here: <http://incidentalcomplexity.com/2017/08/14/July/> We moved to rust from JS to solve some of this, but there were still lingering concerns.

There's nothing really relational about Mech. You lose some of the expressiveness power of Eve, but you gain in performance. The core data structure is a table, and all operations are done on tables of columnar data, so I can get better performance this way.

I studied robotics in grad school which is how I got involved in PL in the first place. I was mostly using MATLAB and was unsatisfied with ROS and C++, so I wanted to make my own language to make robotics development easier. I want it so easy middle and high school students can get involved. I started working on Mech and then found my way to Eve, and now I'm working on Mech again with the lessons learned through working on Eve with Chris and Josh and Rob.

My end goal with Mech is to use it as part of a nonprofit outreach program I'm getting off the ground at Lehigh U. I talk a little about my rationale for the language here if you'd like to take a read: <http://docs.mech-lang.org/#/live2019.mec>

text:
When I read "tools for everyone", I think about the opposite of "tools only usable by experts". I don't mean that everybody should use the tools, but everyone that wants/needs to, can use them. And I neither mean that experts must not use these tools.

Maybe a good example is Max/MSP. Some musicians with no programming skills can use it and begin to program, experts can also find it usefull, especially for the audio processing part of the tool. As an "expert" programmer, I prefer setting audio flows with MaxMSP than JavaScript/WebAudio (or even Faust, which is  a text language designed for audio processing). I just find that for programmers it is sometime frustrating and more complicated than text language. That's why I believe in mixed tools: both visual and text languages.

text: <https://www.case-podcast.org/27-felienne-hermans-on-programming-education>

attachments.title: Felienne Hermans on Programming Education
attachments.text: In this episode, Stefan Tilkov talks with Felienne Hermans, associate Professor at Leiden University in the Netherlands heading the Programming Education Research Lab. They talk about programming education with a focus on people learning their first programming skills and how the current approach in schools and university is flawed. Felienne explains the concept of deliberate practice and code vocalization and answers simple questions like "What's the best programming language for education?"
attachments.fallback: CaSE: Conversations about Software Engineering: Felienne Hermans on Programming Education

text: <@UEQ6M68H0> I think Python rode the wave of the web (and recently the data science and ml boom). To displace one of those 3 languages you mention, I think you'd have to find a big wave to ride and/or have something really simple yet radical. I like what you said here: "I feel the battle is one to supplant both Excel and the top 3 languages, in one tool that finally bridges these two almost uncommunicating realms.". I think of Excel as a 2D language, and have been trying to bridge the world of the 1-D => AST languages with grid languages like Excel. I also think someone could make some wholly novel type of 3-D language in AR or VR that could be a hit

text:
Oh, there's an obscure Japanese film I'm trying to track down.

Wiki: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/My_God,_My_God,_Why_Hast_Thou_Forsaken_Me%3F_(film)>
Trailer: <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=am7YzAiEsVw>

I used to own a copy, which I purchased from a bootleg DVD shop in a Chinatown in 2007-ish. A few years ago I lent it to a friend, they lost it, and I've been unable to find a copy for sale (or otherwise, /cough/) anywhere. I've tried emailing the international distributor (no response), scouring various japanese retail sites via Google Translate (no luck). My next hope is to find someone more familiar with Japanese to see (A) if they know of where to look for such a thing or (B) if they're able to find any leads.

For the better part of a year, I watched this film once a day. It's the sort of thing that I imagine will eventually (if not already) be out of print, and will never be re-released, and will be relegated to the dustbin of history, which is profoundly sad as it's quite an unusual and interesting film.

text:
On a slight tangent, what is *up* with your website? Why does <https://s-ol.nu/ba> redirect to <https://ba.s-ol.nu/articles/mmmfs/:%20text/html+interactive>? Is it a permalink guaranteed to continue to redirect there over time, or is it like the index page of a blog? Can I rely on <https://ba.s-ol.nu/articles/mmmfs> working over time?

Why does clicking on the link lead to a blank page for several seconds before the content pops up? Are you converting from pdf on demand or something?!

Is there somewhere I can read about the facet dropdown and so on?

text: The Newton stuff seems a bit off-topic. Am I missing something? I know that the Newton is interesting as a historical landmark — it was a precursor to many things we have today, egg freckles especially — but I'm not sure what relevance it has to our focus on making new kinds of programming/computation/media tools.

text:
Usually, you’d want to send some things here in #general to #meta instead — let’s try the opposite today: Was just reading this thread in <#CEXED56UR|meta>: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/CEXED56UR/p1577580388049900|https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/CEXED56UR/p1577580388049900>

And it made me think… probably because I was just on Twitter and there I read this thread: <https://twitter.com/Jonathan_Blow/status/1212857853754478592|https://twitter.com/Jonathan_Blow/status/1212857853754478592>

We software developers are an interesting bunch.

> seemed to work.. _fine_. Not pretty, not pleasant, but tolerable.
> 
What makes us so much more tolerant towards weak design than the rest of the world? 
Why do we value features more than experience? 
And do you see this as a problem at all or is this just… fine?

attachments.fallback:
[December 28th, 2019 4:46 PM] ivanreese: I (finally) tried Zulip for the first time today. Here are some initial impressions:

:small_orange_diamond: I too found the UI a bit clunky. Not nearly as rough as Spectrum, probably a bit better than Discourse, but not as good as Slack (or Discord). If we switched to Zulip, we'd be trading a bit of polish for a few more features. That's probably a good trade.

:white_check_mark: Linking directly to a topic, or to a message, seems to work just fine.

:small_orange_diamond: Topics being flattened into each stream seems like a mixed blessing, compared to the nested threads of Slack. If there are two intense conversations happening at the same time, the top-level stream view might become hard to navigate. There are some cases where two people spin a Slack thread up into the 100s of replies, which would also make the All Messages view hard to peruse unless you muted the noisy discussions. So it feels like you have more control, at the cost of needing to micromanage.

:white_check_mark: The performance seems good.

:white_check_mark: Searching the entire history of FoC is powerful!

:white_check_mark: Being able to import all existing history and users from our Slack is powerful!

I think I'd be willing to propose to the community at large that we make the switch. If anyone watching here feels slightly or strongly that we shouldn't, now would be a great time to express those feelings.
attachments.text:
I (finally) tried Zulip for the first time today. Here are some initial impressions:

:small_orange_diamond: I too found the UI a bit clunky. Not nearly as rough as Spectrum, probably a bit better than Discourse, but not as good as Slack (or Discord). If we switched to Zulip, we'd be trading a bit of polish for a few more features. That's probably a good trade.

:white_check_mark: Linking directly to a topic, or to a message, seems to work just fine.

:small_orange_diamond: Topics being flattened into each stream seems like a mixed blessing, compared to the nested threads of Slack. If there are two intense conversations happening at the same time, the top-level stream view might become hard to navigate. There are some cases where two people spin a Slack thread up into the 100s of replies, which would also make the All Messages view hard to peruse unless you muted the noisy discussions. So it feels like you have more control, at the cost of needing to micromanage.

:white_check_mark: The performance seems good.

:white_check_mark: Searching the entire history of FoC is powerful!

:white_check_mark: Being able to import all existing history and users from our Slack is powerful!

I think I'd be willing to propose to the community at large that we make the switch. If anyone watching here feels slightly or strongly that we shouldn't, now would be a great time to express those feelings.

They’re much more maintainable, and they’re easier for emerging economies to build. ...

https://twitter.com/Jonathan_Blow/status/1212857853754478592
We software developers are an interesting bunch.
seemed to work..
fine
. Not pretty, not pleasant, but tolerable.
What makes us so much more tolerant towards weak design than the rest of the world?
Why do we value features more than experience?
And do you see this as a problem at all or is this just… fine?

I think I'd be willing to propose to the community at large that we make the switch. If anyone watching here feels slightly or strongly that we shouldn't, now would be a great time to express those feelings.
attachments.text:
I (finally) tried Zulip for the first time today. Here are some initial impressions:

:small_orange_diamond: I too found the UI a bit clunky. Not nearly as rough as Spectrum, probably a bit better than Discourse, but not as good as Slack (or Discord). If we switched to Zulip, we'd be trading a bit of polish for a few more features. That's probably a good trade.

:white_check_mark: Linking directly to a topic, or to a message, seems to work just fine.

:small_orange_diamond: Topics being flattened into each stream seems like a mixed blessing, compared to the nested threads of Slack. If there are two intense conversations happening at the same time, the top-level stream view might become hard to navigate. There are some cases where two people spin a Slack thread up into the 100s of replies, which would also make the All Messages view hard to peruse unless you muted the noisy discussions. So it feels like you have more control, at the cost of needing to micromanage.

:white_check_mark: The performance seems good.

:white_check_mark: Searching the entire history of FoC is powerful!

:white_check_mark: Being able to import all existing history and users from our Slack is powerful!

I think I'd be willing to propose to the community at large that we make the switch. If anyone watching here feels slightly or strongly that we shouldn't, now would be a great time to express those feelings.
